schemaVersion: Ehel English Course Final Assessment v1.2
subject: English
title: Grade 7 English Final Course Quiz
description: A cumulative assessment of vocabulary, reading, language and communication across Units 1-9.

sections.title: Words and Meaning
sections.description: Use Grade 7 vocabulary accurately in familiar and new contexts.

sections.title: Reading and Language
sections.description: Show reading understanding and control of Grade 7 language patterns.

sections.title: Communication and Application
sections.description: Apply English to speaking, writing and purposeful communication.

questions.question: What does 'belligerent' mean?
questions.options: A strong feeling of dislike or hatred towards someone. | Angry and ready to fight or argue. | To make someone very angry. | Wanting to hurt someone because they hurt you.
questions.correctAnswer: Angry and ready to fight or argue.
questions.explanation: The belligerent dog barked loudly at anyone who came near.

questions.question: What does 'malevolence' mean?
questions.options: A strong feeling of dislike or hatred towards someone. | Someone who is very interested in themselves and thinks they are better than others. | The wish to do evil or harm to others. | To make someone very angry.
questions.correctAnswer: The wish to do evil or harm to others.
questions.explanation: The villain in the story showed malevolence by trying to hurt the hero.

questions.question: What does 'vindictive' mean?
questions.options: A strong feeling of dislike or hatred towards someone. | Someone who is very interested in themselves and thinks they are better than others. | To talk about something in a way that shows you have different opinions and want to convince someone. | Wanting to hurt someone because they hurt you.
questions.correctAnswer: Wanting to hurt someone because they hurt you.
questions.explanation: After being teased, she felt vindictive and wanted to get back at her classmates.

questions.question: What does 'infuriate' mean?
questions.options: A discussion where people express different opinions about something. | Someone who is very interested in themselves and thinks they are better than others. | To make someone very angry. | To talk about something in a way that shows you have different opinions and want to convince someone.
questions.correctAnswer: To make someone very angry.
questions.explanation: It can infuriate me when people talk during a movie.

questions.question: What does 'benevolent' mean?
questions.options: Having a strong desire to achieve something and not giving up. | Kind and generous; wanting to help others. | Showing great excitement and interest about something. | Working hard and being careful to do things well.
questions.correctAnswer: Kind and generous; wanting to help others.
questions.explanation: The benevolent woman donated food to the homeless shelter.

questions.question: What does 'charismatic' mean?
questions.options: Hard-working and very dedicated to tasks. | Having a special charm that attracts and inspires people. | Showing great excitement and interest about something. | Working hard and being careful to do things well.
questions.correctAnswer: Having a special charm that attracts and inspires people.
questions.explanation: The charismatic leader inspired everyone to work together for a better school.

questions.question: What does 'determined' mean?
questions.options: Brave and not afraid of danger or difficulty. | Hard-working and very dedicated to tasks. | Having a strong desire to achieve something and not giving up. | Showing great excitement and interest about something.
questions.correctAnswer: Having a strong desire to achieve something and not giving up.
questions.explanation: She was determined to finish her homework before going out to play.

questions.question: What does 'diligent' mean?
questions.options: Brave and not afraid of danger or difficulty. | Hard-working and very dedicated to tasks. | Very famous and admired, often because of a special story or history. | Working hard and being careful to do things well.
questions.correctAnswer: Working hard and being careful to do things well.
questions.explanation: She is a diligent student who always completes her homework on time.

questions.question: What does 'alliteration' mean?
questions.options: A strong, regular, repeated pattern of sound or movement. | Doing or saying something again and again. | The marks used in writing to separate sentences and clarify meaning, like full stops, commas, and question marks. | The repetition of the same sound at the beginning of several words in a sentence or phrase.
questions.correctAnswer: The repetition of the same sound at the beginning of several words in a sentence or phrase.
questions.explanation: "Isaac Piper picked a peck of pickled peppers" is a famous example of alliteration.

questions.question: What does 'rhyme' mean?
questions.options: A word that has the same ending sound as another word. | Doing or saying something again and again. | The marks used in writing to separate sentences and clarify meaning, like full stops, commas, and question marks. | Using words in a way that is not literal, often to create a special meaning or effect.
questions.correctAnswer: A word that has the same ending sound as another word.
questions.explanation: In the poem, "cat" and "hat" are examples of words that rhyme.

questions.question: What does 'rhythm' mean?
questions.options: A strong, regular, repeated pattern of sound or movement. | A way of comparing two things using the words "like" or "as". | The marks used in writing to separate sentences and clarify meaning, like full stops, commas, and question marks. | Using words in a way that is not literal, often to create a special meaning or effect.
questions.correctAnswer: A strong, regular, repeated pattern of sound or movement.
questions.explanation: The drummer kept a steady rhythm that made everyone want to dance.

questions.question: What does 'repetition' mean?
questions.options: A way of comparing two things using the words "like" or "as". | A way of describing something by saying it is something else, to show a similarity. | Doing or saying something again and again. | Using words in a way that is not literal, often to create a special meaning or effect.
questions.correctAnswer: Doing or saying something again and again.
questions.explanation: In class, we practise our vocabulary through repetition to remember the words better.

questions.quizTitle: Transport, Cities, and Urban Solutions checkpoint
questions.question: What does 'melancholy' mean?
questions.options: A feeling of sadness or being unhappy for a long time. | Able to be easily hurt or harmed, either physically or emotionally. | Careful about possible dangers or problems. | Very happy and excited about something good that has happened.
questions.correctAnswer: A feeling of sadness or being unhappy for a long time.
questions.explanation: After her friend moved away, she felt a deep sense of melancholy.

questions.quizTitle: Transport, Cities, and Urban Solutions checkpoint
questions.question: What does 'exuberant' mean?
questions.options: Able to be easily hurt or harmed, either physically or emotionally. | Careful about possible dangers or problems. | Full of energy, excitement, and happiness. | Very hungry or starving.
questions.correctAnswer: Full of energy, excitement, and happiness.
questions.explanation: The children were exuberant as they played in the park on a sunny day.

questions.quizTitle: Transport, Cities, and Urban Solutions checkpoint
questions.question: What does 'elated' mean?
questions.options: Careful about possible dangers or problems. | Feeling satisfied with yourself and not wanting to improve or change. | Very happy and excited about something good that has happened. | Very hungry or starving.
questions.correctAnswer: Very happy and excited about something good that has happened.
questions.explanation: She was elated when she found out she had won the competition.

questions.quizTitle: Transport, Cities, and Urban Solutions checkpoint
questions.question: What does 'vulnerable' mean?
questions.options: Able to be easily hurt or harmed, either physically or emotionally. | Feeling satisfied with yourself and not wanting to improve or change. | Very hungry or starving. | Very unpleasant or rude; something that annoys people.
questions.correctAnswer: Able to be easily hurt or harmed, either physically or emotionally.
questions.explanation: The little bird was vulnerable because it had just fallen from its nest.

questions.quizTitle: Outdoor Adventures and Environmental Protection checkpoint
questions.question: What does 'acknowledge' mean?
questions.options: A serious effort or attempt to do something. | To accept or admit that something is true, or to recognise that you have received something. | To create or start something that is meant to last. | To show how something works or to prove something clearly.
questions.correctAnswer: To accept or admit that something is true, or to recognise that you have received something.
questions.explanation: I want to acknowledge that you did a great job on your project.

questions.quizTitle: Outdoor Adventures and Environmental Protection checkpoint
questions.question: What does 'collaborate' mean?
questions.options: A serious effort or attempt to do something. | To create or start something that is meant to last. | To think carefully about something to decide how good, important or effective it is. | To work together with others to achieve a common goal.
questions.correctAnswer: To work together with others to achieve a common goal.
questions.explanation: The students will collaborate to create posters about protecting the environment.

questions.quizTitle: Outdoor Adventures and Environmental Protection checkpoint
questions.question: What does 'demonstrate' mean?
questions.options: Important or meaningful; large enough to have an effect. | To create or start something that is meant to last. | To show how something works or to prove something clearly. | To think carefully about something to decide how good, important or effective it is.
questions.correctAnswer: To show how something works or to prove something clearly.
questions.explanation: The ranger will demonstrate how to set up a tent safely.

questions.quizTitle: Outdoor Adventures and Environmental Protection checkpoint
questions.question: What does 'endeavour' mean?
questions.options: A serious effort or attempt to do something. | Important or meaningful; large enough to have an effect. | To find a solution to a problem, or to make a firm decision. | To think carefully about something to decide how good, important or effective it is.
questions.correctAnswer: A serious effort or attempt to do something.
questions.explanation: Learning to navigate with a compass was a worthwhile endeavour.

questions.question: What does 'analogy' mean?
questions.options: A goal or aim that you want to achieve. | A way of explaining something by comparing it to something else that is similar. | An unfair preference or opinion that affects how you think or act. | Facts or information that help prove something is true.
questions.correctAnswer: A way of explaining something by comparing it to something else that is similar.
questions.explanation: The teacher used an analogy to explain how a computer works by comparing it to a brain.

questions.question: What does 'argumentative' mean?
questions.options: A goal or aim that you want to achieve. | A way of thinking about something; a perspective. | Facts or information that help prove something is true. | Someone who likes to argue or disagree with others.
questions.correctAnswer: Someone who likes to argue or disagree with others.
questions.explanation: My friend can be very argumentative when we talk about our favourite movies.

questions.question: What does 'bias' mean?
questions.options: A goal or aim that you want to achieve. | A way of thinking about something; a perspective. | An unfair preference or opinion that affects how you think or act. | The most exciting or important part of a story or event.
questions.correctAnswer: An unfair preference or opinion that affects how you think or act.
questions.explanation: The teacher tried to be fair, but some students felt she had a bias towards the football players.

questions.question: What does 'evidence' mean?
questions.options: A way of thinking about something; a perspective. | Facts or information that help prove something is true. | The most exciting or important part of a story or event. | The situation or background that helps you understand something.
questions.correctAnswer: Facts or information that help prove something is true.
questions.explanation: The detective found evidence that showed who took the missing money.

questions.quizTitle: Success, Careers, and Personal Growth checkpoint
questions.question: What does 'ambition' mean?
questions.options: A strong desire to achieve something important in life. | An important event or stage in the development of something. | Continuing to work hard even when things are difficult or take a long time. | Something that has been accomplished through effort and skill.
questions.correctAnswer: A strong desire to achieve something important in life.
questions.explanation: Her ambition is to become a doctor who helps children in rural areas.

questions.quizTitle: Success, Careers, and Personal Growth checkpoint
questions.question: What does 'dedication' mean?
questions.options: An important event or stage in the development of something. | Being fully committed to a task or purpose; giving time and energy to something. | Excellence that makes someone stand out; a high mark or honour. | Something that has been accomplished through effort and skill.
questions.correctAnswer: Being fully committed to a task or purpose; giving time and energy to something.
questions.explanation: His dedication to studying every evening helped him pass all his exams.

questions.quizTitle: Success, Careers, and Personal Growth checkpoint
questions.question: What does 'perseverance' mean?
questions.options: An important event or stage in the development of something. | Continuing to work hard even when things are difficult or take a long time. | Excellence that makes someone stand out; a high mark or honour. | The ability to recover from difficulties and keep going.
questions.correctAnswer: Continuing to work hard even when things are difficult or take a long time.
questions.explanation: Through perseverance, she learned to speak English fluently.

questions.quizTitle: Success, Careers, and Personal Growth checkpoint
questions.question: What does 'achievement' mean?
questions.options: A job that requires special training or education. | Excellence that makes someone stand out; a high mark or honour. | Something that has been accomplished through effort and skill. | The ability to recover from difficulties and keep going.
questions.correctAnswer: Something that has been accomplished through effort and skill.
questions.explanation: Winning the science prize was her greatest achievement this year.

questions.question: What does 'express' mean?
questions.options: A statement that makes something clear or easy to understand. | To communicate by writing; to be similar to or match something. | To say that something is true, but not prove it yet. | To show or tell what you think or feel.
questions.correctAnswer: To show or tell what you think or feel.
questions.explanation: I want to express my ideas in the school magazine.

questions.question: What does 'claim' mean?
questions.options: A statement that makes something clear or easy to understand. | The power to change or affect someone or something. | To communicate by writing; to be similar to or match something. | To say that something is true, or to ask for something you believe is yours.
questions.correctAnswer: To say that something is true, or to ask for something you believe is yours.
questions.explanation: She will claim her prize after winning the contest.
